fact: February 9 is exactly 40 days after New Year's Day - that's the same amount of time it rained during Noah's flood!
In the Northern Hemisphere, groundhogs are still fast asleep underground on this date, dreaming through their winter nap that won't end for another month.
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier each day around February 9 in most places, which means you're gaining almost a quarter-hour of daylight each week!
February 9 falls smack in the middle of Aquarius season - the water bearer constellation that's actually made of stars, not water (sorry, fish!).
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, February 9 is peak summer heat time, when some desert lizards have to do a crazy hot-foot dance to keep from burning their toes on the sand.
Tree sap in cold places is still frozen solid like sweet popsicles on February 9, which is why maple syrup makers have to wait another month before tapping trees.
The moon on February 9 can be in any phase, but when it's full around this date, some people call it the 'Hunger Moon' because winter food supplies are running low.
February 9 is still 40 days away from the spring equinox - that's longer than most summer vacations!
In really cold places, your breath creates the biggest, puffiest clouds on days like February 9 because the air is super dry and freezing.
Polar bears in the Arctic are having babies right around February 9, but the tiny cubs are only as big as hamsters and can't even see yet!
February 9 is day 40 of the year, which means you've already lived through more than 10% of the entire year - time flies!
In places with snow, February 9 marks the time when snow gets that weird crusty layer on top that's strong enough to hold up a squirrel but will dump a human straight through.
